topic: Area of Study 1: Instrumental Music of Haydn, Mozart and Beethoven
subject: music

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.
The answer examiners want is: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reich. “Glass” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: The real figure is 1952. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
